figures.caption: Figure 4: The DSN of four different points: point A is a corner point, point B is an edge point, point C is a non-feature point near the edge, and point D is a non-feature point on a smooth surface.
Figure 9: Qualitative results of our RWoDSN applied to several models from the ABC dataset and real-scanned models.

Figure 1: The results of the baseline methods and the proposed RWoDSN on three typical models with imbalanced sampling patterns (highlighted by red dotted rectangles), large-scale sharp features, small-scale smooth features (highlighted by green dotted rectangles), and small-scale detailed features (highlighted by magenta dotted rectangles), respectively.
Figure 2: The illustration of the proposed DSN, kNN and the ball neighborhood.

Figure 5: The illustration of vertex_Matrix, di, j r and di, j c : (a) shows the nesting disks, which can be considered as an N × 2π/φ matrix, as shown in (b). (c) shows an example of a non-feature point on a smooth surface, (d) shows an example of a non-feature point located near the edge, and (e) shows an example of an edge point.
Figure 8: The results of the baseline methods compared with the proposed RWoDSN.
Table 1: The quantitative results of the proposed methods and the baselines. The best scores are in bold, and the second-rank scores are underlined.
Table 2: The timings of the baseline methods and the proposed RWoDSN. “-” indicates that no results were obtained.
Figure 7: The results of our RWoDSN using different parameters (with models and ground truth are shown in Fig.9).
Figure 6: The resulting graph-based DSNs of the points in Fig.2.

Figure 3: The illustration of the DSN construction for point p. (a) A series of nested disks centered at point p and perpendicular to the normal vector. (b) The parameters that determine the sampling points on the disks. (c) The bins on the disk plane. (d) The neighboring points of p projected onto the disk plane. (e) The front view and side view of the DSN for point p.
Figure 11: The results of surface segmentation.
Figure 10: The results of our RWoDSN applied to noisy and simplified models.
